title: Palm wax container candle
subtitle: A crystallised pale candle in a glass jar using sustainably sourced palm wax
excerpt: Palm wax produces a natural crystalline pattern on the surface as it cools, giving the finished candle a feathered or snowflake-like appearance unique to this wax. Pour temperature is critical: too hot and the crystals don't form; too cool and the wax pits. Scented with white cedar and vanilla.
sourceNotes: Palm wax crystallisation and pour temperature: Candlescience technical guide to palm wax (2022). Sustainably sourced palm wax: RSPO (Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil) certification reference. Fragrance load at 6% for palm wax: Bramble Berry candle formulation guide (2023). Palm wax shrinkage and second pour: Lone Star Candle Supply technical notes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales easily to multiple jars. Pour all jars from the same batch at the same time to keep the crystallisation consistent.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The candle needs 12 hours undisturbed at room temperature to allow the crystals to form fully before it can be moved.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Palm wax candles keep for several years if stored away from direct heat and light. The fragrance fades after around two years but the wax itself doesn't deteriorate.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which melted wax is poured into the container. Palm wax requires a specific pour temperature window (95 to 100 degrees C) to achieve its natural crystal patterns.
The first time a candle is lit, burn it long enough for the wax pool to reach the full diameter of the jar. This sets the burn pattern for all subsequent uses.

content.text: Palm wax has a higher melting point than soy and a unique crystal-forming property as it cools. When poured at the correct temperature into a glass jar, the wax develops a feathered pattern across the surface, ranging from fine needles to broad snowflake shapes depending on how quickly the jar cools. The crystal pattern cannot be fully controlled, which is part of the appeal. Use sustainably sourced palm wax from an RSPO-certified supplier.

items.prepNote: feathering or crystallising grade, RSPO certified
CD-14 or ECO-6 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Melt wax over a water bath, never directly on a hob. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen fire blanket within reach.

Melt palm wax over a water bath until fully liquid, heating to 105 to 110 degrees C. Palm wax has a higher melting point than soy; it needs to be fully liquid and hot to pour correctly. Warm the glass jar in an oven at 60 degrees C for 5 minutes before pouring; a cold jar causes the wax to contract rapidly and inhibits crystal formation.
Remove the wax from the heat and let it cool to 95 to 100 degrees C. Add the fragrance oil and stir for 2 minutes to fully incorporate.
Centre a pre-tabbed wick in the warm jar and hold it in place with a wick centring bar. Pour the wax at 95 to 100 degrees C. The
is the key variable for palm wax: below 90 degrees C and the crystals form so quickly that the surface pits; above 100 degrees C and the crystals don't form at all.

Leave the candle completely undisturbed at room temperature for at least 12 hours. Moving the jar while the wax is setting disrupts the crystal formation. A slight sink hole around the wick is normal with palm wax; do a small second pour at 95 degrees C to top up if needed.
Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn and before each subsequent burn.

rule: on the first light, burn the candle for long enough that the melted wax pool reaches all the way to the jar edges. For a 6 cm diameter jar, allow 3 to 4 hours. Candles that are extinguished before the pool reaches the edge develop a tunnel down the centre.

Keep away from direct sunlight and heat sources; both degrade the fragrance and soften the wax. The candle keeps several years. The fragrance fades after around two years; the wax and crystal pattern remain.
